summary: The post argues that AI's promise of increased efficiency comes with a dangerous hidden cost: reduced resilience. Drawing on examples from supply chains, Boeing's safety failures, and Jevons Paradox, the author contends that AI-driven efficiency will accelerate resource consumption and increase fragility, precisely the opposite of what humanity needs as it approaches planetary boundaries.
key_points: AI-driven efficiency gains inherently reduce resilience by eliminating safety margins and redundancy
Jevons Paradox means efficiency gains in resource use actually increase aggregate consumption, not decrease it
COVID exposed the fragility of hyper-efficient just-in-time supply chains
Humanity is approaching planetary boundaries and needs resilience, not further acceleration of growth
The author poses an open question: can AI be redirected toward resilience rather than optimization?
money_quotes: On the other side of the efficiency coin is resiliency. As you make anything more efficient, you lose resilience.
When you take efficiency to the extreme, you get deadly fragility.
Nobody is talking about using AI for resiliency. Nobody wants to pay for a technology which will increase their costs in the long run.
Utilizing AI as an approach to increase our efficiency is a kiss of death. We now need resiliency, not efficiency.
Efficiency gains will NOT reduce our usage of fossil fuels in the aggregate. We need fossil fuels to be more expensive to use, not less.
key_insight: AI's core sales pitch of making everything more efficient will paradoxically accelerate resource depletion and increase systemic fragility at the precise moment humanity needs resilience against planetary limits.
